intent:
Open a BIG library that lives on a SLOW EXTERNAL DISK, cold cache, and do
nothing else — no clicking, no watching. This isolates the cost of merely
existing: the ingest walk, the gatekeeper's per-file header read, and the
background gen sweep's first minutes.

What to read:
  - `ingest_io_s` against wall time. The gatekeeper does one 16-byte read
    per candidate file; on a spinning disk that is one ~12ms seek each, so
    a library of thousands turns "listing the files" into minutes of I/O.
    If ingest_io_s is a large fraction of the time to `library_count`, the
    gatekeeper — not the sweep — is what makes a cold open feel dead.
  - Worker jobs, `gen`/`made` row. `worker-s` is how much wall time the
    sweep took out of the pool; p95/max say how long ONE file can pin a
    worker. Three workers × a multi-second job each is the whole pool.
  - Render-thread stalls + phase attribution. The claim under test is that
    the UI thread stalls. If it does, `ingest_install` or `drain_media`
    should own the tail; if neither does, the stall is not in this process
    and the next place to look is Tier B (GPU/present) or the OS.
  - `rss_peak_mb` and `pending_bytes_peak_mb`: the crash canary. A sweep
    that outruns the per-frame upload budget grows the result channel
    without bound, and the RGBA in it is ~1MB per thumb.

This scenario deliberately has NO performance thresholds. `inputs` points
at a real, user-owned library — it is machine-specific and will not run
elsewhere unchanged.
